title: Linking two water butts in series
subtitle: Connecting a second butt to double garden water storage
excerpt: A single 200-litre water butt empties in one or two watering sessions during a dry spell. Linking a second butt in series doubles capacity without additional downpipe connections. When the first fills, overflow goes to the second rather than the drain.
sourceNotes: Waterwise UK garden water conservation guidance. Thames Water garden water use guidance. RHS water-wise gardening guidance on maximising storage capacity.

glossaryTerms.definition: A container (typically 100-400 litres) connected to a downpipe via a diverter kit to collect roof runoff. A 200 litre butt fills from approximately 10-15 mm of rainfall on a typical semi-detached roof (40-50 m² of contributing catchment).
Collecting and storing roof runoff for garden or other uses. Simple water butt systems are domestic-scale; larger underground tanks can supply toilet flushing and outdoor taps.

content.text: holds 200 litres. A medium vegetable garden (20 m²) in a dry week requires 60 to 100 litres every two to three days. Without linking, a full butt empties within two to three waterings and then overflow from the next rain event goes straight to the drain. Linking a second butt captures that overflow. A link kit is inexpensive and the second butt can be positioned anywhere that a short overflow hose can reach.

The overflow outlet on the first butt (typically a 22 mm fitting near the top) connects via a short hose to the inlet of the second butt, fitted through a hole drilled near the top. When the first butt fills, water flows through the overflow into the second. The second butt has its own tap near the base. The second butt does not need a downpipe connection.

The top of the second butt must be at the same height or lower than the overflow outlet of the first. If it is higher, gravity will not drive the flow. Position on a stable, level surface. Both butts should be on a raised stand (200-300 mm) to allow a watering can under the tap.
Step 2, drill the inlet hole in the second butt.

Drill a hole near the top of the second butt to match the link kit fitting diameter (typically 22 mm or 32 mm). Fit the rubber grommet and the push-fit connector from the link kit. The hole must be near the top to maximise storage: a low inlet would limit the second butt to only partial fill.

Remove the existing overflow outlet plug from the first butt and connect the hose to the overflow outlet. Run the hose (trim to length with a utility knife if needed) to the inlet fitting in the second butt. The hose should have a slight fall toward the second butt.
Step 4, divert the second butt's overflow away from the building.
Fit an overflow hose to the second butt's overflow outlet and direct it to a planted bed or drain. Without this, when both butts are full, rain overflow has nowhere to go.

attrs.title: How much catchment you have
attrs.body: The total roof area that drains to the downpipe feeding the first butt determines how quickly both butts fill. Measure the plan area of the roof section, multiply by rainfall in mm to get litres (1 mm rain on 1 m² = 1 litre). A 50 m² roof section with 5 mm of rain yields 250 litres, filling both a 200 litre and 100 litre butt in a single moderate shower. On an average UK roof, two linked butts fill approximately every 10-15 days through the growing season.

content.text: Two linked butts store around 400 litres. A medium garden during a two-week dry spell may need 400 to 600 litres. For larger gardens or dry-climate planting, underground
tanks of 1000 to 5000 litres with a pump provide substantially more capacity but require excavation.
